q: Which of the following is a possible danger of electricity if improperly used?
What two quantities are mentioned as dangerous to humans in the context of electricity?
Which of the following is one of the three possible causes of electricity dangers?
What material is typically used to insulate live and neutral wires in electrical appliances?
What can happen to electrical insulation with time and use, particularly in appliance cables that bend and twist?
a: It deteriorates, causing cracks and breaks.

q: What is the danger if the vulcanised rubber covering the live wire is damaged and exposed?
a: It might cause a mild shock.
It can cause a severe electric shock to the user.
It will simply stop the appliance from working.
It will automatically blow a fuse.

q: What is the second possible cause of electricity dangers?
What can result from a short circuit where the live wire makes electrical contact with the neutral wire due to damaged insulation?
a: A large current and a fire.

q: What is a typical cause of an electrical overload?
a: Overuse of extension cords and multiple plug adapters.
Using appliances with low power ratings.

q: What is the third possible cause of electricity dangers?
Why are damp conditions particularly dangerous when using electrical appliances?
a: Water makes the human body resistance higher.
Water can provide a conducting path for large current.

q: What is the general safety advice regarding electrical appliances and wet hands?
a: It is safe to operate appliances with slightly damp hands.
One should never operate an electrical appliance with wet hands.
Only low-voltage appliances can be used with wet hands.
Switches, plugs, sockets can be damp.

q: What happens to the human body's resistance when it is wet?
What is the consequence if water from a wet floor touches an exposed live wire while a person is in a bath tub?
a: No effect due to water's resistance.

q: Which of these is a common hazard mentioned that should be avoided with wet hands?
